verses.commentary: Some present told Jesus about the Galileans whose blood Pilate had mingled with their sacrifices. Brutal politicized atrocity — Pilate killed pilgrims as they sacrificed. Lukan unique.
'Do you think these Galileans were worse sinners than all other Galileans because they suffered this?' Rejects retributive interpretation.
'No, but unless you repent, you will all likewise perish.' Suffering is not the diagnostic; unrepentance is. Universal call.
Eighteen on whom the tower in Siloam fell — were they worse offenders than all others in Jerusalem?
'No, but unless you repent, you will all likewise perish.' Repeated for emphasis. Theodicy redirected to repentance.
Parable: man had a fig tree in his vineyard. Came to look for fruit, found none. Three years of barrenness.
To the vinedresser: 'Three years I have come seeking fruit and find none. Cut it down. Why should it use the ground?' Time has expired.
Vinedresser: 'Sir, let it alone this year also — I will dig around it, put manure on it.'
'If next year it bears fruit — well; if not, cut it down.' Intercessory delay. Israel's window of repentance dramatized.
Teaching in a synagogue on the Sabbath. The setting matters for the controversy that follows.
A woman with a disabling spirit (pneuma astheneias, πνεῦμα ἀσθενείας) for eighteen years — bent over, could not straighten herself fully. Physical and spiritual affliction merged.
Jesus saw, called her over: 'Woman, you are freed from your disability.'
Laid hands on her. Immediately she stood up straight and glorified God. Touch + word; instant restoration.
Synagogue ruler indignant — Jesus healed on the Sabbath: 'There are six days for work. Come on those days and be healed, not on the Sabbath.' Officious rule-citation.
Lord (ho kyrios, ὁ κύριος): 'Hypocrites! Each of you on the Sabbath unties his ox or his donkey and leads it away to water.' Even Sabbath rules permit animal care.

'This woman, a daughter of Abraham, whom Satan bound for eighteen years — ought she not be loosed from this bond on the Sabbath?' Bondage-and-loosing language. Sabbath is precisely the day for liberation.
His adversaries put to shame; all the people rejoiced at the glorious things he was doing. Public verdict reverses the ruler's.
What is the kingdom of God like? What shall I compare it to?
Like a mustard seed planted in a garden. Grew, became a tree, birds nested in its branches. Tiny start, vast canopy (Daniel 4 / Ezekiel 17 echoes).
Again: To what shall I compare the kingdom of God?
Like leaven a woman took, hid in three measures of flour till it was all leavened. Hidden, pervasive, transformative.
Went on through towns and villages, teaching, journeying toward Jerusalem. The travel narrative momentum maintained.
Someone: 'Lord, will those who are saved be few?'
'Strive (agonizesthe, ἀγωνίζεσθε — agonize) to enter through the narrow door. Many, I tell you, will seek to enter and will not be able.' Effort required; many fail.
When the master rises and shuts the door — you stand outside, knock, saying, 'Lord, open to us' — he answers, 'I do not know where you come from.' Door closes.
Then: 'We ate and drank in your presence, you taught in our streets.' Surface familiarity claimed.
He says: 'I do not know where you come from. Depart from me, all workers of evil!' Echo of Matthew 7:23.
Weeping and gnashing of teeth — when you see Abraham, Isaac, Jacob, and all the prophets in the kingdom of God — and you yourselves cast out.
'People will come from east and west, from north and south, and recline at table in the kingdom of God.' Eschatological banquet, universally peopled.
'Some are last who will be first, some are first who will be last.' Reversal restated.
Some Pharisees: 'Get out and go away from here, for Herod wants to kill you.' Warning offered — perhaps to scare him out of Galilee toward Jerusalem.
'Go tell that fox (alopeki, ἀλώπεκι — sly, cunning), Behold, I cast out demons and perform cures today and tomorrow, and the third day I finish my course.' Herod dismissed as no obstacle.
'I must go on my way today, tomorrow, the day following — for it cannot be that a prophet should perish away from Jerusalem.' Determined trajectory. Jerusalem owns the prophet-killing franchise.

'Jerusalem, Jerusalem — killing the prophets and stoning those sent to it — how often I would have gathered your children as a hen gathers her brood under her wings, and you were not willing.' Lament-cry. Hen image — tender maternal.
'Behold, your house is forsaken. You will not see me until you say, Blessed is he who comes in the name of the Lord' (Psalm 118:26). The temple-house departure foretold. Future recognition required.
